title: How to Use ChatGPT for Code Review (with Prompts)
author: Rahul Singh
summary:
Can ChatGPT actually review code?

ChatGPT can review code. If you paste a function, a class, or even a few hundred lines of source code into the chat window and ask it to find bugs, it will often identify real issues. It can spot null reference errors, missing error handling, SQL injection vulnerabilities, race conditions, and inefficient algorithms. For a general-purpose language model that was not specifically designed for code review, its ability to catch real problems is genuinely impressive.

But "can it review code" and "should you rely on it for code review" are two different questions, and the answer to the second one depends entirely on what you expect from it.

What ChatGPT does well

ChatGPT excels at reviewing isolated code snippets. When you give it a single function or a small module with clear boundaries, it can provide surprisingly thorough feedback. Here is what it handles reliably:

full_text:
Can ChatGPT actually review code?

ChatGPT can review code. If you paste a function, a class, or even a few hundred lines of source code into the chat window and ask it to find bugs, it will often identify real issues. It can spot null reference errors, missing error handling, SQL injection vulnerabilities, race conditions, and inefficient algorithms. For a general-purpose language model that was not specifically designed for code review, its ability to catch real problems is genuinely impressive.

But "can it review code" and "should you rely on it for code review" are two different questions, and the answer to the second one depends entirely on what you expect from it.

 
 
 What ChatGPT does well

ChatGPT excels at reviewing isolated code snippets. When you give it a single function or a small module with clear boundaries, it can provide surprisingly thorough feedback. Here is what it handles reliably:

Bug detection in contained logic - Off-by-one errors, incorrect boolean conditions, missing return statements, uninitialized variables, and type mismatches in dynamically typed languages.

Security pattern recognition - Common vulnerability patterns like SQL injection via string concatenation, cross-site scripting from unsanitized user input, hardcoded credentials, and insecure cryptographic usage.

Code style and readability - Naming conventions, function length, excessive nesting, duplicated code blocks, and unclear control flow.

Language idiom suggestions - It knows the idiomatic way to write code in most popular languages and can suggest more Pythonic, more idiomatic Go, or more modern JavaScript patterns.

Explaining complex code - If you do not understand what a piece of code does, ChatGPT is excellent at walking through it line by line.

 
 
 What ChatGPT cannot do

The limitations are significant and become apparent the moment you try to use ChatGPT as a replacement for a proper code review process:

No repository context. ChatGPT sees only the code you paste into the chat. It does not know about the rest of your codebase, your project's architecture, your database schema, your API contracts, or your deployment environment. A function that looks perfectly fine in isolation might be catastrophically wrong given the context of how it is called.

No diff awareness. In a real code review, you review changes - what was added, what was removed, and why. ChatGPT cannot compare your new code against the previous version. It reviews the code as-is, not the change itself.

No institutional knowledge. It does not know your team's coding conventions, your past decisions, or why certain patterns exist in your codebase. It will suggest "improvements" that violate your team's established practices.

No persistent memory across sessions. Each conversation starts fresh. ChatGPT does not remember what it reviewed yesterday or build up an understanding of your project over time.

Token limits. You cannot paste an entire application into ChatGPT. Depending on the model, you are limited to roughly 8,000 to 128,000 tokens per conversation, which translates to approximately 200 to 3,000 lines of code. For large pull requests that touch many files, this is a hard constraint.

Hallucination risk. ChatGPT sometimes confidently identifies "bugs" that are not bugs, suggests APIs that do not exist, or recommends patterns that introduce new issues. Every suggestion must be verified.

 
 
 Setting realistic expectations

The right mental model for ChatGPT code review is this: it is like asking a knowledgeable colleague who has never seen your project to review a snippet of your code. They will catch obvious problems and offer useful suggestions, but they will miss context-dependent issues and occasionally be wrong. That makes it a useful supplementary tool, not a replacement for human review or dedicated code review platforms.

If you need automated code review that understands your full repository, integrates with your pull request workflow, and provides consistent results across your team, tools like CodeRabbit or PR-Agent are purpose-built for that job. But if you want quick feedback on a specific piece of code, ChatGPT is a genuinely useful option - especially if you use the right prompts.

 
 
 Prompt templates for code review

The quality of ChatGPT's code review depends heavily on how you prompt it. A vague prompt like "review this code" produces vague feedback. A structured prompt that specifies what to look for, how to format the output, and what severity levels to use produces actionable results.

Here are battle-tested prompt templates for different review scenarios. Each one is designed to be copied directly into ChatGPT with your code pasted where indicated.

 
 
 General review prompt

This is the all-purpose prompt for when you want a broad review covering bugs, security, performance, and readability.

You are an expert code reviewer. Review the following code for:

1. **Bugs and logic errors** - incorrect conditions, off-by-one errors, null/undefined handling, race conditions
2. **Security vulnerabilities** - injection flaws, authentication issues, data exposure, insecure defaults
3. **Performance issues** - unnecessary allocations, N+1 queries, missing caching, algorithmic complexity
4. **Readability and maintainability** - naming, function length, code duplication, unclear control flow

For each issue found:
- Describe the problem in one sentence
- Rate the severity: CRITICAL, HIGH, MEDIUM, or LOW
- Show the problematic code
- Provide a corrected version

If the code is well-written, say so. Do not invent issues that do not exist.

Language: [specify language]
Framework: [specify framework if applicable]
Context: [brief description of what this code does]

Code:

 Enter fullscreen mode
 

 Exit fullscreen mode
 

[paste your code here]

 Enter fullscreen mode
 

 Exit fullscreen mode
 

This prompt works because it gives ChatGPT a clear role (expert code reviewer), specific categories to evaluate, a structured output format, and permission to say the code is fine. That last point matters - without it, ChatGPT tends to manufacture issues to seem thorough.

 
 
 Security-focused review prompt

Use this when reviewing authentication, authorization, input handling, or any code that processes user data.

You are a security engineer performing a code audit. Analyze the following
code exclusively for security vulnerabilities.

Check for:
1. **Injection vulnerabilities** - SQL injection, command injection, LDAP injection, XSS
2. **Authentication and authorization flaws** - broken auth, privilege escalation, missing access controls
3. **Data exposure** - sensitive data in logs, hardcoded secrets, insufficient encryption
4. **Input validation** - missing validation, improper sanitization, type confusion
5. **Session management** - insecure session handling, CSRF vulnerabilities, token weaknesses
6. **Cryptographic issues** - weak algorithms, improper key management, predictable randomness
7. **Dependency risks** - known vulnerable patterns, unsafe deserialization

For each finding, provide:
- **Vulnerability**: Name and CWE ID if applicable
- **Location**: The specific line or function
- **Risk**: CRITICAL / HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW
- **Exploit scenario**: How an attacker could exploit this
- **Fix**: Corrected code

Do not flag stylistic issues or non-security concerns.

Language: [specify language]
Code:

 Enter fullscreen mode
 

 Exit fullscreen mode
 

[paste your code here]

 Enter fullscreen mode
 

 Exit fullscreen mode
 

 
 
 Performance review prompt

Use this when you are optimizing hot paths, database queries, or code that runs at scale.

You are a performance engineer. Analyze the following code for performance
issues and optimization opportunities.

Evaluate:
1. **Time complexity** - Are there O(n^2) or worse operations that could be O(n) or O(n log n)?
2. **Memory usage** - Unnecessary object allocations, large data structures held in memory, memory leaks
3. **Database queries** - N+1 queries, missing indexes (inferred from query patterns), unbounded result sets
4. **I/O operations** - Blocking calls that should be async, missing connection pooling, unbatched operations
5. **Caching opportunities** - Repeated computations, redundant API calls, cache-friendly data access patterns
6. **Concurrency** - Thread safety issues, lock contention, parallelization opportunities

For each issue:
- **Problem**: What the performance issue is
- **Impact**: Estimated severity (e.g., "10x slowdown on large datasets" or "linear memory growth")
- **Current code**: The problematic section
- **Optimized code**: Your suggested improvement
- **Trade-offs**: Any downsides to the optimization (readability, complexity, etc.)

Ignore code style and non-performance concerns.

Expected scale: [e.g., "handles 10,000 requests/second" or "processes CSV files up to 1GB"]
Language: [specify language]
Code:

 Enter fullscreen mode
 

 Exit fullscreen mode
 

[paste your code here]

 Enter fullscreen mode
 

 Exit fullscreen mode
 

 
 
 Readability and maintainability prompt

Use this for refactoring sessions or when onboarding new team members to a codebase.

You are a senior developer reviewing code for long-term maintainability.
Focus exclusively on readability, clarity, and maintainability.

Evaluate:
1. **Naming** - Are variable, function, and class names clear and descriptive?
2. **Function design** - Are functions doing one thing? Are they short enough to understand at a glance?
3. **Code duplication** - Is there repeated logic that should be extracted?
4. **Error handling** - Are errors handled consistently and informatively?
5. **Comments and documentation** - Are complex sections explained? Are there misleading comments?
6. **Abstraction level** - Is the code at a consistent level of abstraction within each function?
7. **Testing friendliness** - Is the code structured to be easily testable? Are dependencies injectable?

For each suggestion:
- **Issue**: What makes this code hard to maintain
- **Before**: The current code
- **After**: Your refactored version
- **Rationale**: Why the refactored version is easier to maintain

Do not change the code's behavior. Focus purely on making it clearer for
the next developer who reads it.

Language: [specify language]
Code:

 Enter fullscreen mode
 

 Exit fullscreen mode
 

[paste your code here]

 Enter fullscreen mode
 

 Exit fullscreen mode
 

 
 
 Language-specific prompts

Generic prompts miss language-specific pitfalls. Here are targeted prompts for the three most commonly reviewed languages.

Python code review prompt:

You are a senior Python developer. Review the following Python code for:

1. **Python-specific bugs** - mutable default arguments, late binding closures,
 incorrect use of is vs ==, shallow vs deep copy issues
2. **Type safety** - missing type hints, type hint inconsistencies, Any overuse
3. **Pythonic patterns** - list comprehensions vs loops, context managers,
 generators for large datasets, dataclasses vs dicts
4. **Async issues** - blocking calls in async functions, missing await,
 improper event loop usage
5. **Dependency management** - unsafe imports, circular imports, version pinning

Python version: [specify version]
Framework: [Django/FastAPI/Flask/None]
Code:

 Enter fullscreen mode
 

 Exit fullscreen mode
 

[paste your code here]

 Enter fullscreen mode
 

 Exit fullscreen mode
 

JavaScript/TypeScript code review prompt:

You are a senior JavaScript/TypeScript developer. Review the following code for:

1. **JS/TS-specific bugs** - type coercion traps, prototype pollution,
 closure variable capture in loops, promise rejection handling
2. **TypeScript strictness** - any types that should be narrowed, missing null
 checks, incorrect type assertions, unused generics
3. **Async patterns** - unhandled promise rejections, race conditions in state
 updates, missing error boundaries, callback hell
4. **Memory leaks** - event listener cleanup, closure-retained references,
 timer cleanup in components
5. **Framework patterns** - [React: hook rules, unnecessary re-renders,
 missing deps arrays / Node: middleware ordering, error handling middleware]

Runtime: [Node.js / Browser / Both]
Framework: [React/Next.js/Express/None]
Code:

 Enter fullscreen mode
 

 Exit fullscreen mode
 

[paste your code here]

 Enter fullscreen mode
 

 Exit fullscreen mode
 

Java code review prompt:

You are a senior Java developer. Review the following Java code for:

1. **Java-specific bugs** - null pointer dereferences, incorrect equals/hashCode,
 resource leaks (unclosed streams/connections), ConcurrentModificationException risks
2. **Concurrency issues** - race conditions, improper synchronization,
 non-atomic read-modify-write, deadlock potential
3. **Memory concerns** - unnecessary autoboxing, string concatenation in loops,
 large object retention, static collection growth
4. **API design** - breaking encapsulation, mutable return types,
 checked vs unchecked exceptions, interface segregation
5. **Modern Java** - Optional usage, Stream API vs loops, records vs classes,
 pattern matching (if using Java 17+)

Java version: [specify version]
Framework: [Spring Boot/Quarkus/None]
Code:

 Enter fullscreen mode
 

 Exit fullscreen mode
 

[paste your code here]

 Enter fullscreen mode
 

 Exit fullscreen mode
 

 
 
 Step-by-step: reviewing code with ChatGPT

Knowing the right prompts is only half the process. How you prepare your code, interpret the results, and iterate on feedback determines whether you get useful output or noise.

 
 
 Step 1: Prepare your code

Before pasting code into ChatGPT, do some preparation to maximize the quality of feedback you get.

Scope the review. Do not dump an entire file if you only changed one function. ChatGPT reviews better when the scope is focused. If your change touches multiple files, review each one separately.

Include necessary context. If a function depends on a type definition, an interface, or a configuration value that is defined elsewhere, include those definitions. ChatGPT cannot infer what a UserDTO looks like if you do not tell it.

Remove irrelevant code. Boilerplate imports, auto-generated code, and unchanged surrounding functions add noise without adding context. Strip them out unless they are relevant to the review.

Specify the language and framework. Always state the language, runtime version, and framework. "Review this Python 3.12 FastAPI endpoint" produces much better results than "review this code."

 
 
 Step 2: Choose the right prompt

Select the most appropriate prompt template from the section above based on what you want to focus on:

Starting a new feature? Use the general review prompt.

Working on authentication or data handling? Use the security prompt.

Optimizing a slow endpoint? Use the performance prompt.

Refactoring legacy code? Use the readability prompt.

Working in a specific language? Use the language-specific prompt.

 
 
 Step 3: Interpret the results

ChatGPT will return a list of findings, typically organized by severity or category. Here is how to interpret them effectively:

Trust CRITICAL and HIGH findings conditionally. These are often legitimate issues, but verify each one against your actual codebase. ChatGPT may flag a null pointer issue that is already handled by middleware or a framework feature it does not know about.

Be skeptical of MEDIUM and LOW findings. These are more likely to be stylistic preferences or suggestions that trade one set of trade-offs for another. Evaluate them against your team's conventions.

Watch for hallucinated APIs. ChatGPT sometimes suggests using functions, methods, or libraries that do not exist, or that exist in a different version than what you are using. Always verify that suggested fixes actually compile and run.

Ignore "issues" that are project conventions. If ChatGPT says your variable names should follow a different convention than what your team uses, that is not a bug - it is a preference mismatch.

 
 
 Step 4: Iterate on feedback

One pass is rarely enough. After addressing the initial findings, follow up with targeted questions:

I have addressed the issues you found. Here is the updated code. Are there
any remaining concerns?

[paste updated code]

 Enter fullscreen mode
 

 Exit fullscreen mode
 

You can also drill deeper into specific findings:

You flagged a potential race condition in the updateBalance function.
Can you explain the exact sequence of operations that would cause
this, and suggest a fix using database-level locking?

 Enter fullscreen mode
 

 Exit fullscreen mode
 

This iterative approach is where ChatGPT provides the most value. Each round narrows the focus and produces more specific, actionable feedback.

 
 
 Building a GitHub Actions integration

If you want to move beyond manual copy-paste and automate ChatGPT-based code review in your pull request workflow, you can build a basic integration using the OpenAI API and GitHub Actions. This approach requires writing some glue code, but it gives you automated review on every PR.

 
 
 Basic OpenAI API GitHub Action

Here is a working GitHub Actions workflow that uses the OpenAI API to review pull request diffs and post comments:

name: ChatGPT Code Review

on:
 pull_request:
 types: [opened, synchronize]

permissions:
 contents: read
 pull-requests: write

jobs:
 review:
 runs-on: ubuntu-latest
 steps:
 - name: Checkout code
 uses: actions/checkout@v4
 with:
 fetch-depth: 0

 - name: Get PR diff
 id: diff
 run: |
 DIFF=$(git diff origin/${{ github.event.pull_request.base.ref }}...HEAD -- '*.py' '*.js' '*.ts' '*.java' '*.go' | head -c 12000)
 echo "diff<<DIFF_EOF" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
 echo "$DIFF" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
 echo "DIFF_EOF" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

 - name: Review with OpenAI API
 id: review
 env:
 OPENAI_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.OPENAI_API_KEY }}
 run: |
 RESPONSE=$(curl -s https://api.openai.com/v1/chat/completions \
 -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
 -H "Authorization: Bearer $OPENAI_API_KEY" \
 -d "$(jq -n \
 --arg diff "${{ steps.diff.outputs.diff }}" \
 '{
 model: "gpt-4o",
 messages: [
 {
 role: "system",
 content: "You are a code reviewer. Review the following git diff for bugs, security issues, and performance problems. Be concise. Only flag real issues, not style preferences. Format each issue as: **[SEVERITY]** File:Line - Description"
 },
 {
 role: "user",
 content: ("Review this PR diff:\n\n" + $diff)
 }
 ],
 max_tokens: 2048,
 temperature: 0.1
 }')")
 REVIEW_BODY=$(echo "$RESPONSE" | jq -r '.choices[0].message.content')
 echo "review<<REVIEW_EOF" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
 echo "$REVIEW_BODY" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
 echo "REVIEW_EOF" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

 - name: Post review comment
 uses: actions/github-script@v7
 with:
 script: |
 const review = `${{ steps.review.outputs.review }}`;
 if (review && review !== 'null') {
 await github.rest.issues.createComment({
 owner: context.repo.owner,
 repo: context.repo.repo,
 issue_number: context.issue.number,
 body: `## AI Code Review\n\n${review}\n\n---\n*Automated review powered by OpenAI API*`
 });
 }

 Enter fullscreen mode
 

 Exit fullscreen mode
 

 
 
 What this workflow does

Triggers on PR events - Runs whenever a pull request is opened or updated with new commits.

Extracts the diff - Uses git diff to get only the changed code, filtered to source files. The head -c 12000 truncates large diffs to stay within token limits.

Calls the OpenAI API - Sends the diff to GPT-4o with a system prompt tuned for code review. The low temperature (0.1) reduces randomness for more consistent results.

Posts the result - Creates a PR comment with the review findings.

 
 
 Handling large diffs

The biggest practical challenge with this approach is handling large pull requests. The OpenAI API has token limits, and a diff that exceeds those limits will either be truncated or cause the API call to fail. Here are strategies to deal with this:

File-by-file review. Instead of sending the entire diff at once, loop through changed files and review each one individually. This stays within token limits and produces more focused feedback.

 - name: Review changed files individually
 run: |
 FILES=$(git diff --name-only origin/${{ github.event.pull_request.base.ref }}...HEAD | grep -E '\.(py|js|ts|java|go)$')
 for FILE in $FILES; do
 DIFF=$(git diff origin/${{ github.event.pull_request.base.ref }}...HEAD -- "$FILE" | head -c 8000)
 if [ -n "$DIFF" ]; then
 # Send each file diff to OpenAI API individually
 # Collect results and post as a single comment
 echo "Reviewing $FILE..."
 fi
 done

 Enter fullscreen mode
 

 Exit fullscreen mode
 

Priority filtering. Only review files in security-critical paths (authentication, payment processing, data access) and skip test files, configuration files, and auto-generated code.

Diff summarization. For very large PRs, first send the full file list and ask ChatGPT which files are most likely to contain issues, then review only those files in detail.

 
 
 Cost management

Every API call costs money. At current OpenAI pricing, reviewing a typical pull request with GPT-4o costs roughly $0.01 to $0.10 depending on the size of the diff. For a team that opens 20 PRs per day, that is approximately $6 to $60 per month.

To control costs:

Use gpt-4o-mini for initial screening at roughly one-tenth the cost, then escalate to gpt-4o only for files flagged as potentially problematic.

Set max_tokens conservatively to avoid paying for overly verbose responses.

Skip trivial PRs by adding a condition that checks the number of changed lines and skips reviews for PRs under a threshold (e.g., fewer than 10 changed lines).

Cache results so that pushing a trivial commit to an already-reviewed PR does not re-review unchanged files.

 
 
 The reality check

Building a production-quality ChatGPT code review integration from scratch requires more engineering than the basic example above suggests. You need to handle API rate limits, manage token budgets across large PRs, post inline comments on specific lines (not just a single PR comment), handle API failures gracefully, and maintain the workflow as the OpenAI API evolves.

This is exactly the problem that dedicated tools solve. PR-Agent is an open-source tool that uses the OpenAI API (or Anthropic, Azure, and others) under the hood but handles all of these integration complexities for you. It parses diffs properly, posts inline comments on the correct lines, supports incremental review on new commits, and provides a configuration system for customizing review behavior. If you are going to use the OpenAI API for code review, using PR-Agent as the integration layer rather than building your own is almost always the better choice.

 
 
 ChatGPT vs dedicated code review tools

ChatGPT is a general-purpose language model. Tools like CodeRabbit, PR-Agent, and GitHub Copilot are purpose-built for code review. That difference shows up in every aspect of the experience.

 
 
 ChatGPT vs CodeRabbit

CodeRabbit is the most widely adopted AI code review tool, with over 2 million connected repositories. It installs as a GitHub App and automatically reviews every pull request within minutes.

Repository context. CodeRabbit indexes your full repository and reviews changes in the context of your entire codebase. ChatGPT sees only what you paste.

Diff-native review. CodeRabbit reviews the diff - it knows what changed, what was added, and what was removed. ChatGPT reviews code as-is.

Inline comments. CodeRabbit posts comments on specific lines of the PR diff, exactly like a human reviewer. ChatGPT gives you a text response in a chat window that you must manually map to your code.

Iterative conversation. You can reply to CodeRabbit's comments on a PR and it will respond with context from the full discussion. ChatGPT loses context between sessions.

Zero setup. Install the GitHub App and it works on every PR. ChatGPT requires manual copy-paste or custom GitHub Actions.

ChatGPT is free with a Plus subscription and gives you unlimited reviews on any code. CodeRabbit has a free tier with rate limits and paid plans starting at $24/user/month.

 
 
 ChatGPT vs PR-Agent

PR-Agent by Qodo is an open-source tool that uses the same LLM providers as ChatGPT (OpenAI, Anthropic) but wraps them in a production-grade code review integration.

Same AI, better integration. PR-Agent can use GPT-4o as its underlying model - the same model that powers ChatGPT. The difference is that PR-Agent handles diff parsing, comment placement, and workflow integration automatically.

Self-hostable. PR-Agent runs in your own infrastructure. Your code never leaves your environment, unlike both ChatGPT (where you paste code into OpenAI's servers) and CodeRabbit (a hosted service).

Multiple commands. PR-Agent offers /review, /describe, /improve, /ask, and other commands. ChatGPT offers a single chat interface.

Cost control. With PR-Agent, you pay only your LLM API costs. With ChatGPT Plus, you pay $20/month regardless of usage.

PR-Agent is the right choice if you specifically want to use OpenAI or Anthropic models for code review but need a proper Git platform integration rather than a chat interface.

 
 
 ChatGPT vs GitHub Copilot

GitHub Copilot's code review feature is built directly into the GitHub PR interface. It is the most seamless option for teams already paying for Copilot Enterprise.

Native integration. Copilot reviews appear in the PR review interface alongside human reviews. ChatGPT exists outside your workflow entirely.

Automatic triggering. Copilot can be set as a required reviewer on all PRs. ChatGPT requires manual action.

Limited configurability. Copilot offers minimal customization of review behavior. ChatGPT can be prompted with arbitrary instructions.

Cost. Copilot requires a Copilot Business ($19/user/month) or Enterprise ($39/user/month) plan. ChatGPT Plus is $20/month per user.

The pattern is clear: ChatGPT has the most flexibility in terms of what you can ask it to do (arbitrary prompts, any language, any review angle), but it lacks every integration feature that makes code review practical for teams. Dedicated tools sacrifice some flexibility in exchange for automation, context, and workflow integration.

 
 
 Limitations and when NOT to use ChatGPT for code review

Understanding where ChatGPT fails as a code reviewer is just as important as knowing where it succeeds. These limitations are not minor inconveniences - they are fundamental constraints that determine whether ChatGPT is appropriate for your use case.

 
 
 No codebase context

This is the single biggest limitation. When you paste a function into ChatGPT, it cannot see the rest of your application. It does not know what calls this function, what types the arguments actually are at runtime, what middleware runs before this code executes, or what database schema underlies your queries. It reviews code in a vacuum.

In practice, this means ChatGPT will miss entire categories of bugs: incorrect usage of shared state, violations of application-level invariants, inconsistencies with other parts of the codebase, and integration issues between components. These are often the most important bugs to catch in code review.

 
 
 Inconsistent results

Ask ChatGPT to review the same code twice and you will get different results. It might flag a potential null pointer issue on the first review and miss it entirely on the second. It might suggest using Optional in one session and suggest null checks in another. This inconsistency makes it unreliable as a systematic quality gate.

Dedicated tools produce deterministic results for rule-based checks and use low-temperature settings with structured prompts for AI-based checks, giving significantly more consistent output.

 
 
 Hallucination risks

ChatGPT sometimes invents problems that do not exist. It may claim a function has a race condition when it is only ever called from a single thread. It may warn about a SQL injection vulnerability in code that uses parameterized queries. It may suggest replacing a function call with one that does not exist in the library version you are using.

These hallucinations are not rare edge cases - they happen frequently enough that every piece of ChatGPT's feedback must be independently verified. For experienced developers, this verification overhead is manageable. For junior developers who may not recognize incorrect suggestions, hallucinated advice can introduce new bugs.

 
 
 No incremental review

When you push a new commit to address review feedback, ChatGPT cannot review only the new changes. It has no concept of "I reviewed this code yesterday and you fixed two of the three issues I raised." Every review is a fresh start. Dedicated tools track which issues have been addressed and focus new reviews on new changes.

 
 
 Manual workflow at scale

The copy-paste workflow works for occasional use on a single file. It breaks down completely when you need to review every pull request across a team of 10 developers, each opening multiple PRs per day. There is no way to scale manual ChatGPT review to a team workflow without building significant automation - at which point you are better off using a tool that already provides that automation.

 
 
 When to avoid ChatGPT for code review

Do not use ChatGPT as your primary code review tool if:

You need compliance-grade review. Regulated industries (finance, healthcare, government) require audit trails, consistent review criteria, and documented processes. ChatGPT provides none of these.

You have a large team. The manual workflow does not scale past one or two developers.

You need security assurance. ChatGPT catches surface-level security patterns but misses context-dependent vulnerabilities. Use a dedicated SAST tool for security review.

Your PRs are large. Token limits and lack of diff awareness make ChatGPT a poor choice for PRs touching more than a few files.

You need reproducible results. If two reviewers run the same code through ChatGPT, they will likely get different findings.

 
 
 Cost analysis

Understanding the real cost of using ChatGPT for code review helps you make an informed decision about whether it is worth it compared to dedicated tools.

 
 
 ChatGPT Plus

At $20 per month, ChatGPT Plus gives you access to GPT-4o with generous usage limits. For a single developer doing occasional code reviews, this is the simplest and cheapest option. You get unlimited conversations, the latest model, and no per-review costs.

Cost per review: Effectively $0 after the monthly subscription, assuming you use ChatGPT for other tasks as well.

The catch: Every review is manual. You are paying $20/month plus your time to copy code, write prompts, interpret results, and map findings back to your codebase. At an average of 10 minutes per review for a mid-size function, and 5 reviews per day, that is nearly an hour of daily time spent on manual ChatGPT review operations.

 
 
 OpenAI API

Using the API directly in a GitHub Actions workflow, the cost depends on the model and the size of code being reviewed.

Model

For a team opening 20 PRs per day with an average of 300 changed lines:

GPT-4o: Approximately $0.80 to $1.60 per day, or $24 to $48 per month

GPT-4o mini: Approximately $0.02 to $0.10 per day, or $0.60 to $3 per month

These are the raw API costs. They do not include the engineering time to build and maintain the GitHub Actions integration, handle edge cases, and deal with API failures.

The raw dollar cost of ChatGPT is competitive, but the hidden cost is developer time. When you factor in the manual effort of copying code, crafting prompts, interpreting results, and mapping findings back to pull requests, dedicated tools are almost always more cost-effective for teams.

 
 
 Best practices for using ChatGPT in code review

If you decide to use ChatGPT for code review - whether as your primary tool or as a supplement - these practices will help you get the most value while avoiding the common pitfalls.

 
 
 Combine ChatGPT with linters and static analysis

ChatGPT should never be your only line of defense. Use it alongside deterministic tools that catch issues ChatGPT misses:

Linters (ESLint, Pylint, Clippy) catch syntax issues, unused variables, and style violations deterministically. Let them handle the mechanical checks so your ChatGPT prompts can focus on logic and design.

Static analysis (Semgrep, SonarQube) catches known vulnerability patterns and code smells using rules that produce consistent, reproducible results. They do not hallucinate.

Type checkers (TypeScript, mypy, pyright) catch type errors that ChatGPT may miss or misidentify. A type checker is always right about type compatibility; ChatGPT is often wrong.

The combination works well: linters and type checkers handle the deterministic, well-defined checks, while ChatGPT handles the fuzzy, context-dependent observations that require natural language understanding.

 
 
 Use ChatGPT for learning, not as a sole reviewer

ChatGPT is exceptional as a learning tool for code review. If you are a junior developer, you can paste code into ChatGPT and get explanations of why certain patterns are problematic, what alternatives exist, and how experienced developers would approach the same problem. This educational value is real and significant.

But learning from ChatGPT is different from relying on ChatGPT. The former supplements your skills. The latter creates a dangerous dependency on a tool that is sometimes wrong and always lacks context.

For junior developers: Use ChatGPT to understand code review feedback from human reviewers. When a senior developer leaves a comment you do not understand, ask ChatGPT to explain it. This accelerates learning without creating blind spots.

For senior developers: Use ChatGPT as a rubber duck with opinions. It is useful for catching things you might overlook in routine code, but trust your own judgment when its suggestions conflict with your understanding of the codebase.

 
 
 Verify every suggestion

This cannot be overstated: do not apply ChatGPT's suggestions without understanding and verifying them. For every recommendation:

Understand the reasoning. If ChatGPT suggests a change, make sure you understand why. If the explanation does not make sense, the suggestion is probably wrong.

Check API accuracy. Verify that any suggested functions, methods, or libraries actually exist in the version you are using. ChatGPT frequently suggests APIs from different library versions or entirely fabricated methods.

Test the fix. Apply the suggestion in a branch and run your tests. Do not merge changes based on ChatGPT's recommendation alone.

Consider side effects. ChatGPT optimizes for the code it can see. A change that improves one function may break callers of that function that ChatGPT does not know about.

 
 
 Structure your reviews by concern

Rather than asking ChatGPT for a single comprehensive review, run multiple focused passes with different prompts. A security-focused pass, followed by a performance pass, followed by a readability pass, produces better results than a single "review everything" prompt. Each pass uses a specialized prompt that primes ChatGPT to focus on a specific concern, reducing the chance that it glosses over issues in one category while being thorough in another.

 
 
 Know when to graduate to dedicated tools

If you find yourself doing any of the following, it is time to move to a dedicated code review tool:

Spending more than 30 minutes per day on manual ChatGPT reviews

Building custom scripts to automate the copy-paste workflow

Wishing ChatGPT could see the rest of your codebase

Needing review coverage on every PR, not just the ones you remember to check

Working on a team where multiple people need consistent review feedback

Tools like CodeRabbit and PR-Agent solve all of these problems out of the box. They use the same underlying AI models (or better, purpose-trained ones) and add the integration layer that makes AI code review practical for teams. ChatGPT is a good starting point, but most developers who use it seriously for code review eventually outgrow it.

 
 
 Conclusion

ChatGPT is a genuinely useful tool for code review when used within its limitations. The prompt templates in this guide will help you get structured, actionable feedback on individual code snippets. For quick reviews of isolated functions, security spot-checks, and learning about code quality patterns, it is hard to beat the convenience of pasting code into a chat window and getting instant feedback.

But ChatGPT is not a code review system. It is a language model that you can use for code review, and the difference matters. It lacks repository context, diff awareness, CI/CD integration, consistent results, and team-scale automation. Every minute you spend on manual copy-paste review is a minute a dedicated tool could save you.

If you are just getting started with AI-assisted code review, ChatGPT is a reasonable first step. Use the prompts in this guide, verify the results carefully, and combine ChatGPT with linters and static analysis tools. When you outgrow the manual workflow - and if you do this regularly, you will - look at CodeRabbit for zero-configuration automated review or PR-Agent for a self-hosted, open-source option that uses the same underlying AI models with proper Git platform integration.

 
 
 Frequently Asked Questions

 
 
 Can ChatGPT review code?

Yes. ChatGPT can review code for bugs, security issues, performance problems, and readability concerns. You paste your code into the chat with a review prompt, and it provides feedback. However, it lacks context about your full codebase, previous changes, and project conventions — limitations that dedicated code review tools address.

 
 
 What is the best ChatGPT prompt for code review?

A good starting prompt is: 'Review this code for bugs, security vulnerabilities, and performance issues. For each issue found, explain the problem, its severity, and provide a fix. Code: [paste code]'. For more targeted reviews, specify the language, framework, and what aspects to focus on.

 
 
 Is ChatGPT good enough for code review?

ChatGPT is useful for quick code review on individual files but has significant limitations: no repository context, no diff analysis, no CI/CD integration, manual copy-paste workflow, and inconsistent results across sessions. Dedicated tools like CodeRabbit or PR-Agent are better for team-scale automated review.

 
 
 How much does it cost to use ChatGPT for code review?

ChatGPT Plus costs $20/month for unlimited GPT-4 access. Using the API directly costs roughly $0.01-0.10 per code review depending on code size and model chosen. Dedicated tools like CodeRabbit offer free tiers that may be more cost-effective for team use.

 
 
 Can I integrate ChatGPT with GitHub for automated code review?

Not directly, but you can use the OpenAI API in a GitHub Action to build a basic automated reviewer. However, tools like PR-Agent (which uses OpenAI/Anthropic APIs under the hood) provide a production-ready integration that handles diff parsing, comment threading, and incremental reviews.
